label: fiction
title: The Poison-Pen of Alder Lane
charge: Criminal libel — sending obscene and threatening letters
elements: The letters were sent as charged.
The accused wrote and sent them.

beats.text: The letters themselves are handed to the jury: vile, fluent, on the accused's own brand of notepaper — and signed with her initials.
beats.reveal_note: Who signs a poison-pen letter? The paper is sold in the village shop to half the lane, and initials at the foot of a libel are bait, not a confession.

beats.text: The complainant, tearful and precise: the feud over the boundary fence, the letters on her mat week after week, the shame of the postman knowing her name.
beats.reveal_note: Every word of the suffering could be true — and she wrote the letters herself. Real tears are not proof of authorship; they were the performance that kept the accusation alive.

beats.text: The post office's evidence: stamps invisibly marked and sold only to the complainant. The next letter to arrive bears one — posted while the accused was under continuous observation elsewhere.
beats.reveal_note: The sting closes the case: the complainant's own stamp, on a letter the accused could not have posted. The victim was the author all along.

beats.text: The village schoolmistress, who taught the accused her letters forty years ago: the woman's hand is laboured and schoolroom-round. 'She could no more sustain that flowing script than fly.'
beats.reveal_note: Quiet corroboration: the letters' fluent copperplate was always beyond her.

beats.text: The judge invites the jury to ask one question: who benefits from a signature on an anonymous letter — the person it names, or the person it points away from?
beats.reveal_note: The question that unravels it: initials on a libel are evidence of a snare.

twist: The letters were signed with her initials, on her brand of notepaper — which is precisely the problem: poison pens don't sign. Marked stamps sold only to the complainant turned up on a letter posted while the accused was watched elsewhere. The tears in the witness box were real; so was the pen in the same hand.
